slides.title: Your First Python Program
slides.body: Every programmer starts here — a tiny program that makes the computer display a message. One line of code, and you're officially a Python programmer.
slides.tip: Hello, World! is a tradition going back to the 1970s — you're joining a long line of coders.

slides.title: Meet print()
slides.body: `print()` is Python's megaphone — whatever you put inside the parentheses gets displayed on your screen. It's the most-used command you'll ever type.

slides.title: Quotes Make It Text
slides.body: Wrapping your message in quotes tells Python *this is text, not a command*. In Python, quoted text is called a **string**. Both `'single'` and `"double"` quotes work.
slides.tip: Forget the quotes and Python panics with a SyntaxError — always wrap your text!

slides.title: Print Multiple Items at Once
slides.body: Separate items with a **comma** inside `print()` and Python joins them with a space automatically. Stack multiple `print()` calls to get multiple lines.
slides.code:
print("Hello", "World", "from Python!")
print("I am learning Python.")
slides.output:
Hello World from Python!
I am learning Python.

slides.title: You Did It — You're a Pythonista
slides.body: You now know `print()`, **strings**, and how to display text on screen. Every Python expert started with exactly this. The whole language opens up from here.
slides.tip: Next up: storing values in variables so your programs can remember things.
